examples.title: Programmed I/O Simulation - CPU Polling Device
examples.description: Demonstrates programmed I/O where the CPU actively polls a device register to check if data is ready, using busy-wait approach.
examples.code:
# Programmed I/O Simulation - CPU Polling Method
# In real hardware, this would read from device registers via memory-mapped I/O

import time
import random

class IODevice:
    """Simulates a simple I/O device with status and data registers"""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.status_register = 0b0  # Bit 0: Ready, Bit 1: Error
        self.data_register = 0
        self.buffer = []
    
    def simulate_data_arrival(self):
        """Simulate external data arriving at device"""
        time.sleep(random.uniform(0.5, 2.0))
        self.data_register = random.randint(1, 100)
        self.status_register |= 0b1  # Set Ready bit
    
    def read_status(self):
        """Read device status register (memory-mapped I/O)"""
        return self.status_register
    
    def read_data(self):
        """Read data from device (clears ready bit)"""
        data = self.data_register
        self.data_register = 0
        self.status_register &= 0b10  # Clear Ready bit
        return data

def programmed_io_transfer(device, num_transfers):
    """
    Programmed I/O: CPU actively polls device status
    CPU is busy-waiting, wasting cycles
    """
    results = []
    
    for i in range(num_transfers):
        # Start data generation on device
        device.simulate_data_arrival()
        
        # CPU POLLS - busy wait until device is ready
        while True:
            status = device.read_status()
            if status & 0b1:  # Check Ready bit
                break
            # CPU wastes cycles here polling
            pass
        
        # Read data from device
        data = device.read_data()
        results.append(data)
        print(f"Transfer {i+1}: Received data = {data}")
    
    return results

# Demonstration
device = IODevice()
print("=== Programmed I/O Demo (Polling) ===")
results = programmed_io_transfer(device, 3)
print(f"Total items transferred: {len(results)}")
examples.explanation: This example simulates programmed I/O where: 1) The CPU continuously polls the device status register (busy-wait loop), 2) The CPU is blocked and cannot do other work while waiting, 3) Data transfer only happens when the device sets the Ready bit, 4) This is inefficient as CPU cycles are wasted in polling. In real systems, memory-mapped I/O allows CPU to read device registers like memory locations.

examples.title: Interrupt-Driven I/O Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates interrupt-driven I/O where the CPU performs other tasks and is notified via callback when device data is ready.
examples.code:
# Interrupt-Driven I/O Simulation
# Uses callback mechanism to simulate hardware interrupts

import time
import random
from threading import Thread, Event

class InterruptController:
    """Simulates Interrupt Request (IRQ) handling"""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.interrupt_handlers = {}
        self.irq_enabled = True
    
    def register_handler(self, irq_line, handler):
        """Register interrupt service routine (ISR)"""
        self.interrupt_handlers[irq_line] = handler
    
    def raise_interrupt(self, irq_line):
        """Simulate hardware raising an interrupt"""
        if self.irq_enabled and irq_line in self.interrupt_handlers:
            print(f"  [INTERRUPT] IRQ {irq_line} raised!")
            self.interrupt_handlers[irq_line]()

class IODeviceInterrupt:
    """I/O device that generates interrupts when data is ready"""
    
    def __init__(self, controller, irq_line):
        self.controller = controller
        self.irq_line = irq_line
        self.data_ready = False
        self.data = 0
    
    def generate_data(self):
        """Simulate asynchronous data arrival"""
        time.sleep(random.uniform(0.5, 2.0))
        self.data = random.randint(100, 999)
        self.data_ready = True
        # Raise interrupt to notify CPU
        self.controller.raise_interrupt(self.irq_line)

def main():
    print("=== Interrupt-Driven I/O Demo ===\n")
    
    # Setup interrupt controller
    pic = InterruptController()
    
    # Shared buffer for data
    data_buffer = []
    transfer_complete = Event()
    
    # Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) - executed on interrupt
    def data_ready_isr():
        """Handle data ready interrupt"""
        nonlocal device
        if device.data_ready:
            data_buffer.append(device.data)
            device.data_ready = False
            print(f"  [ISR] Data handled: {data_buffer[-1]}")
    
    # Register ISR for IRQ 3
    device = IODeviceInterrupt(pic, irq_line=3)
    pic.register_handler(3, data_ready_isr)
    
    # CPU performs useful work while waiting for interrupts
    print("CPU: Starting background tasks...")
    
    # Simulate starting device data generation
    worker = Thread(target=device.generate_data)
    worker.start()
    
    # CPU does other work (simulated)
    for i in range(3):
        print(f"CPU: Doing useful work (task {i+1})...")
        time.sleep(0.8)
    
    worker.join()
    
    print(f"\nCPU: All transfers complete!")
    print(f"Buffer contents: {data_buffer}")
    print("\nAdvantage: CPU was free to do other work while waiting!")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
examples.explanation: This demonstrates interrupt-driven I/O: 1) The device generates an interrupt (IRQ) when data is ready, 2) The CPU registers an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) to handle the interrupt, 3) While waiting, the CPU can perform other useful work instead of polling, 4) The interrupt controller (like PIC in x86) routes interrupts to appropriate handlers, 5) This is more efficient than programmed I/O as CPU utilization is higher. Modern systems use Advanced Interrupt Controllers (APIC) for scalability.

examples.title: DMA Transfer Simulation - Memory to Memory Copy
examples.description: Demonstrates Direct Memory Access (DMA) where data is transferred directly between memory and device without CPU intervention, freeing CPU for other tasks.
examples.code:
# DMA (Direct Memory Access) Transfer Simulation
# Demonstrates how DMA controllers transfer data without CPU overhead

import time
import random
from threading import Thread, Event

class DMAController:
    """
    Simulates a DMA Controller
    In real hardware: Intel 8237 DMA Controller
    """
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.registers = {
            'source': 0,
            'destination': 0,
            'count': 0,
            'control': 0
        }
        self.memory = {}
    
    def setup_transfer(self, source_addr, dest_addr, byte_count):
        """CPU programs DMA registers (one-time setup)"""
        self.registers['source'] = source_addr
        self.registers['destination'] = dest_addr
        self.registers['count'] = byte_count
        self.registers['control'] = 0b1  # Start transfer
        print(f"[CPU] Programmed DMA: {source_addr} -> {dest_addr}, {byte_count} bytes")
    
    def perform_transfer(self):
        """DMA performs the actual data transfer (bus master)"""
        source = self.registers['source']
        dest = self.registers['destination']
        count = self.registers['count']
        
        # Simulate DMA bus mastering and transfer
        for i in range(count):
            # Read from source (memory or I/O device)
            data = self.memory.get(source + i, 0)
            # Write to destination
            self.memory[dest + i] = data
            
        print(f"[DMA] Transfer complete: {count} bytes copied")
        return True

class CPU:
    """CPU that sets up DMA and does other work"""
    
    def __init__(self, dma):
        self.dma = dma
        self.work_done = 0
    
    def setup_dma_transfer(self, source, dest, size):
        """CPU programs DMA controller (minimal work)"""
        self.dma.setup_transfer(source, dest, size)
    
    def do_useful_work(self, duration):
        """CPU performs other tasks while DMA works"""
        print(f"[CPU] Performing useful work...")
        for i in range(duration):
            self.work_done += 1
            time.sleep(0.1)
        print(f"[CPU] Completed {self.work_done} units of work")

def main():
    print("=== DMA Transfer Demo ===\n")
    
    # Initialize DMA and CPU
    dma = DMAController()
    cpu = CPU(dma)
    
    # Initialize source memory with data
    SOURCE_ADDR = 1000
    DEST_ADDR = 2000
    BLOCK_SIZE = 5
    
    print("Initializing source memory...")
    for i in range(BLOCK_SIZE):
        dma.memory[SOURCE_ADDR + i] = random.randint(1, 99)
    
    print(f"Source memory: {[dma.memory[SOURCE_ADDR + i] for i in range(BLOCK_SIZE)]}")
    print(f"Dest memory before DMA: {[dma.memory.get(DEST_ADDR + i, 0) for i in range(BLOCK_SIZE)]}")
    
    # CPU programs DMA (very fast operation)
    cpu.setup_dma_transfer(SOURCE_ADDR, DEST_ADDR, BLOCK_SIZE)
    
    # Start DMA transfer in separate thread (simulates bus mastering)
    dma_thread = Thread(target=dma.perform_transfer)
    dma_thread.start()
    
    # CPU does useful work concurrently
    print("\n--- CPU works in parallel with DMA ---")
    cpu.do_useful_work(4)
    
    # Wait for DMA to complete
    dma_thread.join()
    
    # Verify transfer
    print(f"\nDest memory after DMA: {[dma.memory[DEST_ADDR + i] for i in range(BLOCK_SIZE)]}")
    print("\nDMA Advantage: CPU did useful work while data was transferred!")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
examples.explanation: This demonstrates DMA (Direct Memory Access): 1) CPU programs DMA controller once with source, destination, and transfer size, 2) DMA controller 'bus masters' - takes control of system bus, 3) Data transfers directly between memory and device without CPU intervention, 4) CPU is free to perform other useful work during transfer, 5) When complete, DMA raises interrupt to notify CPU. Common DMA modes: Single Transfer, Block Transfer, Cascade Mode. Used by disk I/O, network cards, sound cards for high-throughput transfers.
